---
title: "Homework 3: Medicare Advantage Star Ratings & Market Share"
author: "Valerie Hernandez"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 1.2
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

> **GitHub Repository:** [https://github.com/valerie-hdz/homework3](https://github.com/valerie-hdz/homework3)

---

# Question 1 Summary Statistics by Year

The table below reports the mean star rating (among rated plans), mean enrollment, mean market share, and total number of plan-year observations for each year from 2010 to 2015.

| Year | Mean Star Rating | Mean Enrollment | Mean Market Share | Number of Plans |
|------|-----------------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| 2010 | 2.964 | 252.245 | 0.065 | 112,856 |
| 2011 | 3.241 | 336.362 | 0.085 | 70,184 |
| 2012 | 3.329 | 372.366 | 0.084 | 69,770 |
| 2013 | 3.525 | 384.188 | 0.080 | 70,930 |
| 2014 | 3.771 | 431.075 | 0.078 | 65,095 |
| 2015 | 3.940 | 466.571 | 0.077 | 67,505 |

: Summary Statistics for Rated MA Plans, 2010–2015 {#tbl-q1}

---

# Question 2: Plans Without a Star Rating

The tablemsummarizes the mean enrollment, mean market share, and total number of plans for the unrated plans by year.

| Year | Mean Enrollment | Mean Market Share | Number of Plans |
|------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| 2010 | 109.950 | 0.067 | 49,536 |
| 2011 | 172.682 | 0.091 | 12,763 |
| 2012 | 162.435 | 0.053 | 8,743 |
| 2013 | 182.393 | 0.050 | 3,246 |
| 2014 | 248.227 | 0.043 | 3,324 |
| 2015 | 312.343 | 0.041 | 4,729 |

: Summary Statistics for Unrated MA Plans, 2010–2015 {#tbl-q2}

---

# Question 3:Distribution of Star Ratings

```{python}
#| label: fig-q3
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of Star Ratings for MA Plans (2010, 2012, 2015)"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
from pathlib import Path

BASE = Path("../../..")
OUT_DIR = BASE / "data" / "output"

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4), sharey=False)
years_to_plot = [2010, 2012, 2015]
colors = ["#4C72B0", "#55A868", "#C44E52"]

for ax, year, color in zip(axes, years_to_plot, colors):
    df = pd.read_csv(OUT_DIR / f"ma_data_{year}_final.csv", low_memory=False)
    rated = df[df["Star_Rating"].notna()]
    counts = (rated["Star_Rating"]
              .value_counts()
              .reindex([2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0], fill_value=0)
              .sort_index())
    ax.bar([str(s) for s in counts.index], counts.values, color=color,
           edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.5)
    ax.set_title(str(year), fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("Star Rating", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_ylabel("Number of Plans", fontsize=10)
    ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"{int(x):,}"))
    ax.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The distributions show how the ratings shifted upward from from 2010 to 2012 to 2015. By 2015, the distribution had shifted toward higher ratings compared to 2010, with fewer plans receiving 2- or 2.5-star ratings and a much larger amount at 3.5 stars and above (right skew for 2010).

---

# Question 4: OLS Regression of Market Share on Star Ratings

| Variable | 2010 | 2011 | 2012 | 2013 | 2014 | 2015 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intercept (≤2.5 stars) | 0.0623 | 0.0689 | 0.0568 | 0.0427 | 0.0497 | 0.0586 |
| | (0.001) | (0.0018) | (0.0021) | (0.0024) | (0.0048) | (0.0038) |
| 3 Stars | 0.004 | 0.0288 | 0.0549 | 0.0544 | 0.0218 | 0.0202 |
| | (0.0017) | (0.0023) | (0.0025) | (0.0029) | (0.0051) | (0.0046) |
| 3.5 Stars | 0.0034 | 0.022 | 0.0368 | 0.046 | 0.0425 | 0.0325 |
| | (0.0019) | (0.0027) | (0.0029) | (0.0027) | (0.0049) | (0.0041) |
| 4 Stars | -0.0001 | 0.0083 | 0.0038 | 0.0244 | 0.0247 | 0.0144 |
| | (0.0021) | (0.0031) | (0.0036) | (0.0033) | (0.0051) | (0.004) |
| 4.5+ Stars | -0.0053 | -0.0068 | 0.0134 | 0.0333 | 0.0241 | 0.0269 |
| | (0.0027) | (0.003) | (0.003) | (0.003) | (0.0051) | (0.0041) |
| N | 20,409 | 20,493 | 22,577 | 25,531 | 24,530 | 23,920 |
| R² | 0.0007 | 0.0126 | 0.0294 | 0.0168 | 0.0070 | 0.0050 |

: OLS Regression of Market Share on Star Rating Indicators (SE in parentheses) {#tbl-q4}

In 2010, star rating indicators show little relationship with market share, and higher-rated plans (4+ stars) have slightly negative coefficients relative to the omitted group but this changed by 2010-2013.

---

# Question 5: Running Variable and Plans Near Star Rating Thresholds

Using 2010 data

| Star Rating | Cutoff | Plans Below Cutoff | Plans Above Cutoff | Total Plans |
|-------------|--------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 3-star | 2.75 | 8,681 | 4,578 | 13,259 |
| 3.5-star | 3.25 | 4,578 | 3,201 | 7,779 |
| 4-star | 3.75 | 3,864 | 1,690 | 5,554 |
| 4.5-star | 4.25 | 3,435 | 194 | 3,629 |

: Plans Around Each Star Rating Threshold (2010) {#tbl-q5}

Across all thresholds, more plans fall below each cutoff than above it.

---

# Question 6: RD Estimates at Bandwidth = 0.125

| Threshold | Estimate | 95% CI Lower | 95% CI Upper |
|-----------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| 3-star vs. 2.5-star | -0.0324 | -0.0523 | -0.0124 |
| 3.5-star vs. 3-star | -0.0711 | -0.0964 | -0.0458 |

: RD Estimates at Bandwidth = 0.125 (Outcome: Market Share) {#tbl-q6}

---

# Question 7: Bandwidth Sensitivity (repeating for various bandwidths)

 Repeating the RD estimation across a range of bandwidths from 0.1, 0.12,0.13,0.14 and 0.15.

| Bandwidth | 3-star vs. 2.5-star | 3.5-star vs. 3-star |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 0.10 | 0.0086 | -0.0503 |
| 0.12 | -0.0227 | -0.0825 |
| 0.125 | -0.0324 | -0.0711 |
| 0.13 | -0.0257 | -0.0697 |
| 0.14 | -0.0039 | -0.0598 |
| 0.15 | 0.0732 | -0.0852 |

: RD Bandwidth Sensitivity Analysis {#tbl-q7}
```{python}
#| label: fig-q7
#| fig-cap: "Bandwidth Sensitivity of RD Estimates"
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bandwidths = [0.10, 0.12, 0.125, 0.13, 0.14, 0.15]
est_3star  = [0.0086, -0.0227, -0.0324, -0.0257, -0.0039, 0.0732]
est_35star = [-0.0503, -0.0825, -0.0711, -0.0697, -0.0598, -0.0852]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

for ax, ests, label, color in zip(
    axes,
    [est_3star, est_35star],
    ["3-star vs. 2.5-star", "3.5-star vs. 3-star"],
    ["#4C72B0", "#C44E52"]
):
    ax.plot(bandwidths, ests, marker="o", color=color, linewidth=2)
    ax.axhline(0, linestyle="--", color="gray", linewidth=0.8)
    ax.axvline(0.125, linestyle=":", color="black", linewidth=0.8, label="h = 0.125")
    ax.set_title(label, fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("Bandwidth", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_ylabel("RD Estimate", fontsize=10)
    ax.legend(fontsize=9)
    ax.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The 3.5-star threshold estimates are consistently negative across all bandwidths and can imply the findings are reasonably stable. On the other hand, the 3-star threshold estimates are sensitive to bandwidth choice, and change sign at 0.10 and 0.15.

---

# Question 8: Examining Whether Contracts Appear to Manipulate Running Variable

```{python}
#| label: fig-q8
#| fig-cap: "Density of Raw Rating Near Star Rating Cutoffs"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 4

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pathlib import Path
from scipy.stats import gaussian_kde

BASE = Path("../../..")
OUT_DIR = BASE / "data" / "output"

ma_2010 = pd.read_csv(OUT_DIR / "ma_data_2010_final.csv", low_memory=False)
ma_2010["mkt_share"] = ma_2010["enrollment"] / ma_2010["avg_enrolled"]

measure_vars_2010 = [
    "breastcancer_screen","rectalcancer_screen","cv_diab_cholscreen",
    "glaucoma_test","monitoring","flu_vaccine","pn_vaccine",
    "physical_health","mental_health","osteo_test","physical_monitor",
    "primaryaccess","osteo_manage","diab_healthy","bloodpressure",
    "ra_manage","copd_test","bladder","falling","nodelays",
    "doctor_communicate","carequickly","customer_service",
    "overallrating_care","overallrating_plan","complaints_plan",
    "appeals_timely","appeals_review","leave_plan","audit_problems",
    "hold_times","info_accuracy","ttyt_available",
]
cols_present = [c for c in measure_vars_2010 if c in ma_2010.columns]
ma_2010["raw_rating"] = ma_2010[cols_present].mean(axis=1, skipna=True)
ma_2010_rd = ma_2010[ma_2010["mkt_share"].notna()].copy()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))

configs = [
    (2.5, 3.0, 2.75, "3-star vs. 2.5-star (cutoff = 2.75)", "#4C72B0"),
    (3.0, 3.5, 3.25, "3.5-star vs. 3-star (cutoff = 3.25)", "#C44E52"),
]

for ax, (lower, upper, cutoff, title, color) in zip(axes, configs):
    sub = ma_2010_rd[
        ma_2010_rd["Star_Rating"].isin([lower, upper]) &
        ma_2010_rd["raw_rating"].notna()
    ]["raw_rating"].dropna()

    left  = sub[sub < cutoff]
    right = sub[sub >= cutoff]

    for side, c in [(left, color), (right, color)]:
        if len(side) > 1:
            kde = gaussian_kde(side, bw_method=0.3)
            xs = np.linspace(side.min(), side.max(), 200)
            ax.plot(xs, kde(xs), color=c, linewidth=2)
            ax.fill_between(xs, kde(xs), alpha=0.15, color=c)

    ax.axvline(cutoff, color="black", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.2, label=f"Cutoff = {cutoff}")
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel("Raw Rating", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_ylabel("Density", fontsize=9)
    ax.legend(fontsize=8)
    ax.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The plots don't show discontinuous jumps at either threshold, so there's no strong sign of score manipulation by plans.

---

# Question 9: Examining Whether Plans Just Above the Threshold Values Have Different Characteristics than Those Below Threshold Values

| Characteristic | Threshold | Coefficient | P-value | N |
|----------------|-----------|-------------|---------|---|
| HMO Plan | 3-star vs. 2.5-star | 0.6009 | < 0.001 | 5,865 |
| Has Part D | 3-star vs. 2.5-star | 0.4309 | < 0.001 | 5,865 |
| HMO Plan | 3.5-star vs. 3-star | -0.5531 | < 0.001 | 2,049 |
| Has Part D | 3.5-star vs. 3-star | -0.0431 | 0.203 | 2,049 |

: Covariate Balance at Star Rating Thresholds (Bandwidth = 0.125) {#tbl-q9}

At both thresholds, HMO status shows large, significant imbalances, indicating that plans just above the threshold differ from those below threshold values. Part D coverage is also imbalanced at the 3-star cutoff,but not at the 3.5-star cutoff.

---

# Question 10: Summary of RD Findings

**RD Estimates at Bandwidth = 0.125**

| Threshold | Estimate | CI Lower | CI Upper |
|-----------|----------|----------|----------|
| 3-star vs. 2.5-star | -0.0324 | -0.0523 | -0.0124 |
| 3.5-star vs. 3-star | -0.0711 | -0.0964 | -0.0458 |

: RD Estimates at Bandwidth = 0.125 {#tbl-q10a}

**Bandwidth Sensitivity**

| Bandwidth | 3-star vs. 2.5-star | 3.5-star vs. 3-star |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 0.10 | 0.0086 | -0.0503 |
| 0.12 | -0.0227 | -0.0825 |
| 0.125 | -0.0324 | -0.0711 |
| 0.13 | -0.0257 | -0.0697 |
| 0.14 | -0.0039 | -0.0598 |
| 0.15 | 0.0732 | -0.0852 |

: Bandwidth Sensitivity Analysis {#tbl-q10b}

**Covariate Balance**

| Characteristic | Threshold | Coefficient | P-value |
|----------------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| HMO Plan | 3-star vs. 2.5-star | 0.6009 | < 0.001 |
| Has Part D | 3-star vs. 2.5-star | 0.4309 | < 0.001 |
| HMO Plan | 3.5-star vs. 3-star | -0.5531 | < 0.001 |
| Has Part D | 3.5-star vs. 3-star | -0.0431 | 0.203 |

: Covariate Balance at Thresholds (Bandwidth = 0.125) {#tbl-q10c}

The regression discontinuity estimates suggest that increasing star ratings is associated with lower enrollment at the cutoffs examined, especially at the 3.5-star threshold. Based on the results, the 3.5-star threshold shows a relatively stable negative effect whereas the 3-star rating threshold results are sensitive to the bandwidth.
